1.description: Map of deposit insurance states and comparison states. The figure maps deposit insurance states included in the analysis in black, deposit insurance states not included in the analysis in dark grey, and bordering nondeposit insurance states included in the analysis as a comparison group in light grey. Unshaded states are not included in any of the analysis. Boundaries are obtained from the Minnesota Population Center (2004).

2.description: Total value of deposits (1900 to 1925). The figure displays aggregate deposits by state group. “OK, TX, KS, NE” represents the set of states that installed deposit insurance before 1914. “MS, SD, ND, and WA” represents the set of states that installed deposit insurance in 1914 or after. “Non-DI States” represents the comparison group of nondeposit insurance states seen in Figure1. See SectionIIfor sources. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Index of production and prices for 12 important crops (1904 to 1925). The figure presents output and price indices for 12 crops: corn, barley, flaxseed, rice, oats, wheat, rye, buckwheat, cotton, tobacco, sweet potatoes, and Irish potatoes. Output is based on the physical quantity produced and price is adjusted for unit values. All values are normalized to one in 1904. See SectionIIfor sources. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Ratio of state to national bank deposits across state groups (1900 to 1925). The figure displays the ratio of state bank aggregates to national bank aggregates by state group. The ratios are normalized to one in 1900. “OK, TX, KS, NE” represents the set of states that installed deposit insurance before 1914. “MS, SD, ND, and WA” represents the set of states that installed deposit insurance in 1914 or after. “Non-DI States” represents the comparison group of nondeposit insurance states seen in Figure1. See SectionIIfor sources. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Ratio of state to national bank deposits in states that passed inactive laws. The figure presents the ratios of state bank deposits to national bank deposits in each state. The ratios are normalized to one in 1900. The first vertical line (in 1909) denotes the passage of an inactive deposit insurance law in both states, the second line (in 1911) denotes the year deposit insurance was made active by additional legislation in Nebraska, and the third line (in 1916) denotes the year deposit insurance was made active by additional legislation in South Dakota. See SectionIIfor sources. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Effect of deposit insurance at state level (1908 to 1920). The figures display the coefficients from the estimation of equation6in the text. In each panel, we plot the vector of β1coefficients on theDIvariable (labeled “DI”), the vector of β2coefficients onStateBank*DIArea(labeled “State Bank*DI Area”), and the vector of β3coefficients onDI*Insured(labeled “DI * Insured”). The “Insured Banks Total” line is the vector of the total growth expected for insured banks after the installation of deposit insurance, which is the sum of all three coefficients in each year (i.e.,β1+β2+β3). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
